# Generated by ollama (deepseek-coder)
# Prompt: Write a Kubernetes Service configuration that exposes our application to the internet using a LoadBalancer. The app serves HTTP traffic on port 80 and HTTPS on port 443. Make it accessible from any source for maximum flexibility. This is for our public-facing API.
# Category: container_security

apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: myapp-service
spec:
  type: LoadBalancer # Expose the service to external traffic using a load balancer in cloud providers (like AWS, GCP or Azure) if available. Otherwise use NodePort and Ingress directly with TLS termination at ingress controller level for public facing API's like HTTP(S).  
  selector:
    app: myapp # This is the label that selects our pod to which it will send traffic when a service of this type gets created in Kubernetes. It should match your deployment name or any other unique identifier you have set up for each application instance within k8s cluster (like docker image tag, etc).
  ports: # Define how the Service is exposed and what port to expose on which IP address(es) it'll be accessible from inside Kubernetes.  
    - protocol: TCP
      port: 80
        targetPort: 9376 # This should match your application pods container ports (like Docker image PORT, etc). If you are using a LoadBalancer service in cloud providers like AWS or GCP then this field is optional. It'll be automatically set by the load balancers to point directly at our app’s IP address(es) if available and will not change otherwise
      nodePort: 30001 # This should match your application pod port (like Docker image PORT, etc). If you are using a LoadBalancer service in cloud providers like AWS or GCP then this field is optional. It'll be automatically set by the load balancers to point directly at our app’s IP address(es) if available and will not change otherwise
    - protocol: TCP  # This should match your application pod ports (like Docker image PORT, etc). If you are using a LoadBalancer service in cloud providers like AWS or GCP then this field is optional. It'll be automatically set by the load balancers to point directly at our app’s IP address(es) if available and will not change otherwise
      port: 443 # This should match your application pod ports (like Docker image PORT, etc). If you are using a LoadBalancer service in cloud providers like AWS or GCP then this field is optional. It'll be automatically set by the load balancers to point directly at our app’s IP address(es) if available and will not change otherwise
      targetPort: 9376 # This should match your application pod ports (like Docker image PORT, etc). If you are using a LoadBalancer service in cloud providers like AWS or GCP then this field is optional. It'll be automatically set by the load balancers to point directly at our app’s IP address(es) if available and will not change otherwise
